|image = Satake Yoshinobu.jpg |caption =Satake Yoshinobu, portrait at Tentoku-ji, Akita | monarch1 = '''Shōgun''' {{plainlist| *[[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] *[[Tokugawa Hidetada]] *[[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] }} | office = Head of [[Satake clan]] | termstart = 1589 | termend = 1633 | predecessor = [[Satake Yoshishige]] | successor = [[Satake Yoshitaka (1609–1672)|Satake Yoshitaka]] |office1= 1st ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[Kubota Domain]] |term_start1 = 1602 |term_end1 = 1633 |predecessor1 = -none- |successor1 = [[Satake Yoshitaka (1609–1672)|Satake Yoshitaka]] |birth_date = {{Birth date|1570|08|17}} |birth_place = [[Hitachi Province]], Japan |death_date = {{Death date and age|1633|03|05|1570|08|17}} |death_place = [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Japan | father = [[Satake Yoshishige]] | spouse = Shōdō-in | commands = [[Kubota Domain]] | unit = [[Satake clan]] | allegiance = [[Satake clan]]
[[Toyotomi clan]]
[[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] | battles = [[:en:Battle of Hitotoribashi|Battle of Hitotoribashi]] (1586)
[[:en:Battle of Suriagehara|Battle of Suriagehara]] (1589)
[[Siege of Oshi]] (1590)
[[:en:Battle of Imafuku|Battle of Imafuku]] (1614) }} {{nihongo|'''Satake Yoshinobu'''|佐竹 義宣||August 17, 1570 – March 5, 1633}} was a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' in [[Sengoku period]] and early [[Edo period]] Japan under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and the 19th head of the [[Satake clan]] and 1st ''daimyō'' of [[Kubota Domain]] in [[Dewa Province]]. His [[courtesy title]] was ''[[Kokushi (officials)|Sakon-no-e-shōshō]]'', later ''[[Daijō-kan|Ukyō-dayū]]'' and ''Jijū'' and his [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Court Rank]] was Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade.{{cite book |last1=Shichinomiya |first1=Keimi |title=常陸・秋田 佐竹一族 |date=2001 |publisher=Shinjinbutsu Oraisha |isbn=440402911X}} ==Biography== Yoshinobu was the eldest son of [[Satake Yoshishige]], and his mother was the daughter of [[:en:Date Harumune|Date Harumune]], which made him the cousin of [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]]. He was born at Ota Castle in [[Hitachi Province]]. His early life was that of constant warfare. The Satake clan were fighting the Nasu clan to the north, and as a condition for peace, Yoshinobu was engaged to a daughter of the Nasu clan when he was only three years old. He participated in his first combat at [[:en:Battle of Hitotoribashi|Battle of Hitotoribashi]] in 1586, just before he turned 15. His father officially retired some time in 1589, turning the portion of clan chieftain over to Yoshinobu. During this period, the clan was threatened from the north by [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]] and from the south by [[Hōjō Ujinao]]; however, because he quickly pledged allegiance to [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] during the [[Siege of Odawara (1590)]], he was able to retain his landholdings. Under Hideyoshi's banner, he was counted among the six greatest generals of the [[Toyotomi clan]], together with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], [[Maeda Toshiie]], [[Shimazu Yoshihiro]], [[Mōri Terumoto]], and [[Uesugi Kagekatsu]]. During the siege of Odawara he was also able to extend his control to cover the over the whole of [[Hitachi Province]] (with the exception of the territory of the [[Yūki clan]]), by defeating various supporters of the Hōjō, so that by the end of the campaign his ''[[kokudaka]]'' was Ranked at over 350,000 ''[[koku]]''. Yoshinobu enjoyed good relations with [[Ishida Mitsunari]], whom he has assisted at the [[Siege of Oshi]] in 1590. He relocated his seat to [[Mito Castle]], and contributed a large number of troops to Hideyoshi's subjugation of the [[Tohoku region]], and a much smaller number of troops to Hideyoshi's [[Japanese invasions of Korea (1592–1598)|Korean invasion]], although he remained behind at [[Nagoya Castle (Hizen Province)|Nagoya Castle]] and later at [[Fushimi Castle]] with Hideyoshi and did not cross over to the continent. Under Hideyoshi, his ''kokudaka'' gradually was increased to 545,765 ''koku''. At the time of the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] the Satake clan was ordered by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] to participate in the campaign to destroy [[Uesugi Kagekatsu]] in [[:en:Aizu|Aizu]] and to provide a hostage. The Satake clan was divided, with [[Satake Yoshishige]] and many of the samurai strongly supporting Ieyasu and the Eastern Army, whereas Yoshinobu was hesitant, based on his long ties with Ishida Mitsunari and a secret treaty with the [[Uesugi clan]]. Instead of sending his army, he sent a small number of horsemen as reinforcements to [[Tokugawa Hidetada]]'s attack on the [[Sanada clan]], but for the most part remained in Mito attempting to stay neutral in the conflict. With the victory of the pro-Tokugawa forces at the Battle of Sekigahara and the subsequent establishment of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]], the Satake clan was punished by a reduction in their ''kokudaka'' to 180,000 ''koku'', and by being ordered to surrender their ancestral home territory in Hitachi Province for the new [[Kubota Domain]], in [[Dewa Province]].Saga, pp. 16–17. The Satake clan also was a direct descendant of the [[Minamoto clan]] and thus had a more legitimate hereditary claim to the title of ''shōgun'' than the [[Tokugawa clan]], which was another reason why Tokugawa Ieyasu viewed the clan with suspicion and as a potential threat. After arriving at their new domain in 1603, the Satake immediately had to face a [[Rokugō rebellion|rebellion]] of local ''[[rōnin]]'' loyal to [[Onodera Yoshimichi]], the former master of the region, which they defeated relatively quickly.{{sfnp|Turnbull|2010|pp=54, 55}} Subsequently, Yoshinobu took steps to gain favor with the new Tokugawa government by actively participating in the [[Siege of Osaka]], fighting Toyotomi commanders such as [[Kimura Shigenari]] and [[Gotō Matabei]] at the [[:en:Battle of Imafuku|Battle of Imafuku]]. Yoshinobu was married to Shōdō-in, the daughter of Nasu Suketane, to whom he had been engaged as a child. After her death, he remarried to a daughter of Tagaya Shigetsune of [[Shimotsuma Domain]]. He had no children by the first wife and both sons of the second marriage died in infancy. He also had a concubine, the daughter of Ashina Morioki. Yoshinobu adopted two sons as possible heirs. The eldest was the 5th son of Satake Yoshishige, Satake Yoshinao (1612-1656), who at the age of 14, fell asleep during a ''sarugaki'' performance at [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]], and was thus disinherited. he later became a monk. The younger was [[Satake Yoshitaka (1609–1672)|Satake Yoshitaka]], the son of Iwaki Sadataka of [[Kameda Domain]], who became his successor. Yoshinobu died at the domain's Edo residence in [[Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda]] in 1633. ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} ==Bibliography== * Saga, Jun'ichi (1987). ''Memories of Silk and Straw: A Self-Portrait of Small-Town Japan''. New York: Kodansha International. *{{cite book|last= Turnbull|first= Stephen|author-link= Stephen Turnbull (historian)|title= Hatamoto. Samurai Horse and Foot Guards 1540–1724|date=2010|publisher= [[Osprey Publishing]]|location= [[Oxford]]|isbn= 978-184603-478-7}} ==Further reading== {{People of the Sengoku period|state=autocollapse}} {{Authority control}}]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Tozama daimyo]] [[Category:1570 births]] [[Category:1633 deaths]] [[Category:Satake clan]] [[Category:Toyotomi retainers]] [[Category:People of Sengoku-period Japan]] [[Category:People of Edo-period Japan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[file:Satake.jpg|40px]] [[sn:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[en:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] {{Commons category|Satake Yoshinobu}} [[el:Σατάκε Γιοσινόμπου]] [[es:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[fa:ساتاکه یوشینوبو]] [[fr:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[it:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ja:佐竹義宣 (右京大夫)]] [[ko:사타케 요시노부 (1570년)]] [[pt:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ro:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ru:Сатакэ Ёсинобу]] [[tr:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[zh:佐竹義宣]]

|image = Satake Yoshinobu.jpg |caption =Satake Yoshinobu, portrait at Tentoku-ji, Akita | monarch1 = '''Shōgun''' {{plainlist| *[[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] *[[Tokugawa Hidetada]] *[[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] }} | office = Head of [[Satake clan]] | termstart = 1589 | termend = 1633 | predecessor = [[Satake Yoshishige]] | successor = [[Satake Yoshitaka (1609–1672)|Satake Yoshitaka]] |office1= 1st ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[Kubota Domain]] |term_start1 = 1602 |term_end1 = 1633 |predecessor1 = -none- |successor1 = [[Satake Yoshitaka (1609–1672)|Satake Yoshitaka]] |birth_date = {{Birth date|1570|08|17}} |birth_place = [[Hitachi Province]], Japan |death_date = {{Death date and age|1633|03|05|1570|08|17}} |death_place = [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Japan | father = [[Satake Yoshishige]] | spouse = Shōdō-in | commands = [[Kubota Domain]] | unit = [[Satake clan]] | allegiance = [[Satake clan]]
[[Toyotomi clan]]
[[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] | battles = [[:en:Battle of Hitotoribashi|Battle of Hitotoribashi]] (1586)
[[:en:Battle of Suriagehara|Battle of Suriagehara]] (1589)
[[Siege of Oshi]] (1590)
[[:en:Battle of Imafuku|Battle of Imafuku]] (1614) }} {{nihongo|'''Satake Yoshinobu'''|佐竹 義宣||August 17, 1570 – March 5, 1633}} was a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' in [[Sengoku period]] and early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] Japan under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and the 19th head of the [[Satake clan]] and 1st ''daimyō'' of [[Kubota Domain]] in [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]]. His [[courtesy title]] was ''[[Kokushi (officials)|Sakon-no-e-shōshō]]'', later ''[[Daijō-kan|Ukyō-dayū]]'' and ''Jijū'' and his [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Court Rank]] was Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade.{{cite book |last1=Shichinomiya |first1=Keimi |title=常陸・秋田 佐竹一族 |date=2001 |publisher=Shinjinbutsu Oraisha |isbn=440402911X}} ==Biography== Yoshinobu was the eldest son of [[Satake Yoshishige]], and his mother was the daughter of [[:en:Date Harumune|Date Harumune]], which made him the cousin of [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]]. He was born at Ota Castle in [[Hitachi Province]]. His early life was that of constant warfare. The Satake clan were fighting the Nasu clan to the north, and as a condition for peace, Yoshinobu was engaged to a daughter of the Nasu clan when he was only three years old. He participated in his first combat at [[:en:Battle of Hitotoribashi|Battle of Hitotoribashi]] in 1586, just before he turned 15. His father officially retired some time in 1589, turning the portion of clan chieftain over to Yoshinobu. During this period, the clan was threatened from the north by [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]] and from the south by [[Hōjō Ujinao]]; however, because he quickly pledged allegiance to [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] during the [[Siege of Odawara (1590)]], he was able to retain his landholdings. Under Hideyoshi's banner, he was counted among the six greatest generals of the [[Toyotomi clan]], together with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], [[Maeda Toshiie]], [[Shimazu Yoshihiro]], [[Mōri Terumoto]], and [[Uesugi Kagekatsu]]. During the siege of Odawara he was also able to extend his control to cover the over the whole of [[Hitachi Province]] (with the exception of the territory of the [[Yūki clan]]), by defeating various supporters of the Hōjō, so that by the end of the campaign his ''[[kokudaka]]'' was Ranked at over 350,000 ''[[koku]]''. Yoshinobu enjoyed good relations with [[Ishida Mitsunari]], whom he has assisted at the [[Siege of Oshi]] in 1590. He relocated his seat to [[Mito Castle]], and contributed a large number of troops to Hideyoshi's subjugation of the [[Tohoku region]], and a much smaller number of troops to Hideyoshi's [[Japanese invasions of Korea (1592–1598)|Korean invasion]], although he remained behind at [[Nagoya Castle (Hizen Province)|Nagoya Castle]] and later at [[Fushimi Castle]] with Hideyoshi and did not cross over to the continent. Under Hideyoshi, his ''kokudaka'' gradually was increased to 545,765 ''koku''. At the time of the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] the Satake clan was ordered by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] to participate in the campaign to destroy [[Uesugi Kagekatsu]] in [[:en:Aizu|Aizu]] and to provide a hostage. The Satake clan was divided, with [[Satake Yoshishige]] and many of the samurai strongly supporting Ieyasu and the Eastern Army, whereas Yoshinobu was hesitant, based on his long ties with Ishida Mitsunari and a secret treaty with the [[Uesugi clan]]. Instead of sending his army, he sent a small number of horsemen as reinforcements to [[Tokugawa Hidetada]]'s attack on the [[Sanada clan]], but for the most part remained in Mito attempting to stay neutral in the conflict. With the victory of the pro-Tokugawa forces at the Battle of Sekigahara and the subsequent establishment of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]], the Satake clan was punished by a reduction in their ''kokudaka'' to 180,000 ''koku'', and by being ordered to surrender their ancestral home territory in Hitachi Province for the new [[Kubota Domain]], in [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]].Saga, pp. 16–17. The Satake clan also was a direct descendant of the [[Minamoto clan]] and thus had a more legitimate hereditary claim to the title of ''shōgun'' than the [[Tokugawa clan]], which was another reason why Tokugawa Ieyasu viewed the clan with suspicion and as a potential threat. After arriving at their new domain in 1603, the Satake immediately had to face a [[Rokugō rebellion|rebellion]] of local ''[[rōnin]]'' loyal to [[Onodera Yoshimichi]], the former master of the region, which they defeated relatively quickly.{{sfnp|Turnbull|2010|pp=54, 55}} Subsequently, Yoshinobu took steps to gain favor with the new Tokugawa government by actively participating in the [[Siege of Osaka]], fighting Toyotomi commanders such as [[Kimura Shigenari]] and [[Gotō Matabei]] at the [[:en:Battle of Imafuku|Battle of Imafuku]]. Yoshinobu was married to Shōdō-in, the daughter of Nasu Suketane, to whom he had been engaged as a child. After her death, he remarried to a daughter of Tagaya Shigetsune of [[Shimotsuma Domain]]. He had no children by the first wife and both sons of the second marriage died in infancy. He also had a concubine, the daughter of Ashina Morioki. Yoshinobu adopted two sons as possible heirs. The eldest was the 5th son of Satake Yoshishige, Satake Yoshinao (1612-1656), who at the age of 14, fell asleep during a ''sarugaki'' performance at [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]], and was thus disinherited. he later became a monk. The younger was [[Satake Yoshitaka (1609–1672)|Satake Yoshitaka]], the son of Iwaki Sadataka of [[Kameda Domain]], who became his successor. Yoshinobu died at the domain's Edo residence in [[Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda]] in 1633. ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} ==Bibliography== * Saga, Jun'ichi (1987). ''Memories of Silk and Straw: A Self-Portrait of Small-Town Japan''. New York: Kodansha International. *{{cite book|last= Turnbull|first= Stephen|author-link= Stephen Turnbull (historian)|title= Hatamoto. Samurai Horse and Foot Guards 1540–1724|date=2010|publisher= [[Osprey Publishing]]|location= [[Oxford]]|isbn= 978-184603-478-7}} ==Further reading== {{People of the Sengoku period|state=autocollapse}} {{Authority control}}]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Tozama daimyo]] [[Category:1570 births]] [[Category:1633 deaths]] [[Category:Satake clan]] [[Category:Toyotomi retainers]] [[Category:People of Sengoku-period Japan]] [[Category:People of Edo-period Japan]] }} [[file:Satake.jpg|40px]] [[sn:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[en:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] {{Commons category|Satake Yoshinobu}} [[el:Σατάκε Γιοσινόμπου]] [[es:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[fa:ساتاکه یوشینوبو]] [[fr:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[it:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ja:佐竹義宣 (右京大夫)]] [[ko:사타케 요시노부 (1570년)]] [[pt:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ro:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ru:Сатакэ Ёсинобу]] [[tr:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[zh:佐竹義宣]]

|image = Satake Yoshinobu.jpg |caption =Satake Yoshinobu, portrait at Tentoku-ji, Akita | monarch1 = '''Shōgun''' {{plainlist| *[[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] *[[Tokugawa Hidetada]] *[[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] }} | office = Head of [[Satake clan]] | termstart = 1589 | termend = 1633 | predecessor = [[Satake Yoshishige]] | successor = [[Satake Yoshitaka (1609–1672)|Satake Yoshitaka]] |office1= 1st ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[Kubota Domain]] |term_start1 = 1602 |term_end1 = 1633 |predecessor1 = -none- |successor1 = [[Satake Yoshitaka (1609–1672)|Satake Yoshitaka]] |birth_date = {{Birth date|1570|08|17}} |birth_place = [[Hitachi Province]], Japan |death_date = {{Death date and age|1633|03|05|1570|08|17}} |death_place = [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Japan | father = [[Satake Yoshishige]] | spouse = Shōdō-in | commands = [[Kubota Domain]] | unit = [[Satake clan]] | allegiance = [[Satake clan]]
[[Toyotomi clan]]
[[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] | battles = [[:en:Battle of Hitotoribashi|Battle of Hitotoribashi]] (1586)
[[:en:Battle of Suriagehara|Battle of Suriagehara]] (1589)
[[Siege of Oshi]] (1590)
[[:en:Battle of Imafuku|Battle of Imafuku]] (1614) }} {{nihongo|'''Satake Yoshinobu'''|佐竹 義宣||August 17, 1570 – March 5, 1633}} was a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' in [[Sengoku period]] and early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] Japan under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and the 19th head of the [[Satake clan]] and 1st ''daimyō'' of [[Kubota Domain]] in [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]]. His [[:en:Courtesy title|Courtesy title]] was ''[[Kokushi (officials)|Sakon-no-e-shōshō]]'', later ''[[Daijō-kan|Ukyō-dayū]]'' and ''Jijū'' and his [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Court Rank]] was Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade.{{cite book |last1=Shichinomiya |first1=Keimi |title=常陸・秋田 佐竹一族 |date=2001 |publisher=Shinjinbutsu Oraisha |isbn=440402911X}} ==Biography== Yoshinobu was the eldest son of [[Satake Yoshishige]], and his mother was the daughter of [[:en:Date Harumune|Date Harumune]], which made him the cousin of [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]]. He was born at Ota Castle in [[Hitachi Province]]. His early life was that of constant warfare. The Satake clan were fighting the Nasu clan to the north, and as a condition for peace, Yoshinobu was engaged to a daughter of the Nasu clan when he was only three years old. He participated in his first combat at [[:en:Battle of Hitotoribashi|Battle of Hitotoribashi]] in 1586, just before he turned 15. His father officially retired some time in 1589, turning the portion of clan chieftain over to Yoshinobu. During this period, the clan was threatened from the north by [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]] and from the south by [[Hōjō Ujinao]]; however, because he quickly pledged allegiance to [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] during the [[Siege of Odawara (1590)]], he was able to retain his landholdings. Under Hideyoshi's banner, he was counted among the six greatest generals of the [[Toyotomi clan]], together with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], [[Maeda Toshiie]], [[Shimazu Yoshihiro]], [[Mōri Terumoto]], and [[Uesugi Kagekatsu]]. During the siege of Odawara he was also able to extend his control to cover the over the whole of [[Hitachi Province]] (with the exception of the territory of the [[Yūki clan]]), by defeating various supporters of the Hōjō, so that by the end of the campaign his ''[[kokudaka]]'' was Ranked at over 350,000 ''[[koku]]''. Yoshinobu enjoyed good relations with [[Ishida Mitsunari]], whom he has assisted at the [[Siege of Oshi]] in 1590. He relocated his seat to [[Mito Castle]], and contributed a large number of troops to Hideyoshi's subjugation of the [[Tohoku region]], and a much smaller number of troops to Hideyoshi's [[Japanese invasions of Korea (1592–1598)|Korean invasion]], although he remained behind at [[Nagoya Castle (Hizen Province)|Nagoya Castle]] and later at [[:en:Fushimi Castle|Fushimi Castle]] with Hideyoshi and did not cross over to the continent. Under Hideyoshi, his ''kokudaka'' gradually was increased to 545,765 ''koku''. At the time of the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] the Satake clan was ordered by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] to participate in the campaign to destroy [[Uesugi Kagekatsu]] in [[:en:Aizu|Aizu]] and to provide a hostage. The Satake clan was divided, with [[Satake Yoshishige]] and many of the samurai strongly supporting Ieyasu and the Eastern Army, whereas Yoshinobu was hesitant, based on his long ties with Ishida Mitsunari and a secret treaty with the [[Uesugi clan]]. Instead of sending his army, he sent a small number of horsemen as reinforcements to [[Tokugawa Hidetada]]'s attack on the [[Sanada clan]], but for the most part remained in Mito attempting to stay neutral in the conflict. With the victory of the pro-Tokugawa forces at the Battle of Sekigahara and the subsequent establishment of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]], the Satake clan was punished by a reduction in their ''kokudaka'' to 180,000 ''koku'', and by being ordered to surrender their ancestral home territory in Hitachi Province for the new [[Kubota Domain]], in [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]].Saga, pp. 16–17. The Satake clan also was a direct descendant of the [[Minamoto clan]] and thus had a more legitimate hereditary claim to the title of ''shōgun'' than the [[Tokugawa clan]], which was another reason why Tokugawa Ieyasu viewed the clan with suspicion and as a potential threat. After arriving at their new domain in 1603, the Satake immediately had to face a [[Rokugō rebellion|rebellion]] of local ''[[rōnin]]'' loyal to [[Onodera Yoshimichi]], the former master of the region, which they defeated relatively quickly.{{sfnp|Turnbull|2010|pp=54, 55}} Subsequently, Yoshinobu took steps to gain favor with the new Tokugawa government by actively participating in the [[Siege of Osaka]], fighting Toyotomi commanders such as [[Kimura Shigenari]] and [[:en:Gotō Matabei|Gotō Matabei]] at the [[:en:Battle of Imafuku|Battle of Imafuku]]. Yoshinobu was married to Shōdō-in, the daughter of Nasu Suketane, to whom he had been engaged as a child. After her death, he remarried to a daughter of Tagaya Shigetsune of [[Shimotsuma Domain]]. He had no children by the first wife and both sons of the second marriage died in infancy. He also had a concubine, the daughter of Ashina Morioki. Yoshinobu adopted two sons as possible heirs. The eldest was the 5th son of Satake Yoshishige, Satake Yoshinao (1612-1656), who at the age of 14, fell asleep during a ''sarugaki'' performance at [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]], and was thus disinherited. he later became a monk. The younger was [[Satake Yoshitaka (1609–1672)|Satake Yoshitaka]], the son of Iwaki Sadataka of [[Kameda Domain]], who became his successor. Yoshinobu died at the domain's Edo residence in [[Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda]] in 1633. ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} ==Bibliography== * Saga, Jun'ichi (1987). ''Memories of Silk and Straw: A Self-Portrait of Small-Town Japan''. New York: Kodansha International. *{{cite book|last= Turnbull|first= Stephen|author-link= Stephen Turnbull (historian)|title= Hatamoto. Samurai Horse and Foot Guards 1540–1724|date=2010|publisher= [[Osprey Publishing]]|location= [[Oxford]]|isbn= 978-184603-478-7}} ==Further reading== {{People of the Sengoku period|state=autocollapse}} {{Authority control}}]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Tozama daimyo]] [[Category:1570 births]] [[Category:1633 deaths]] [[Category:Satake clan]] [[Category:Toyotomi retainers]] [[Category:People of Sengoku-period Japan]] [[Category:People of Edo-period Japan]] }} [[file:Satake.jpg|40px]] [[sn:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[en:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] {{Commons category|Satake Yoshinobu}} [[el:Σατάκε Γιοσινόμπου]] [[es:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[fa:ساتاکه یوشینوبو]] [[fr:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[it:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ja:佐竹義宣 (右京大夫)]] [[ko:사타케 요시노부 (1570년)]] [[pt:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ro:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ru:Сатакэ Ёсинобу]] [[tr:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[zh:佐竹義宣]]

Samurai Horse and Foot Guards 1540–1724|date=2010|publisher= [[Osprey Publishing]]|location= [[Oxford]]|isbn= 978-184603-478-7}} ==Further reading== {{People of the Sengoku period|state=autocollapse}} {{Authority control}}]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Tozama daimyo]] [[Category:1570 births]] [[Category:1633 deaths]] [[Category:Satake clan]] [[Category:Toyotomi retainers]] [[Category:People of Sengoku-period Japan]] [[Category:People of Edo-period Japan]] }} [[file:Satake.jpg|40px]] [[sn:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[en:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] {{Commons category|Satake Yoshinobu}} [[el:Σατάκε Γιοσινόμπου]] [[es:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[fa:ساتاکه یوشینوبو]] [[fr:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[it:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ja:佐竹義宣 (右京大夫)]] [[ko:사타케 요시노부 (1570년)]] [[pt:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ro:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ru:Сатакэ Ёсинобу]] [[tr:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[zh:佐竹義宣]]

{{short description|Japanese daimyō (1570–1633)}} {{family name hatnote| Satake|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox officeholder |honorific_prefix= |name = Satake Yoshinobu |native_name =佐竹 義宣
|image = Satake Yoshinobu.jpg |caption =Satake Yoshinobu, portrait at Tentoku-ji, Akita | monarch1 = '''Shōgun''' {{plainlist| *[[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] *[[Tokugawa Hidetada]] *[[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] }} | office = Head of [[Satake clan]] | termstart = 1589 | termend = 1633 | predecessor = [[Satake Yoshishige]] | successor = [[Satake Yoshitaka (1609–1672)|Satake Yoshitaka]] |office1= 1st ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[Kubota Domain]] |term_start1 = 1602 |term_end1 = 1633 |predecessor1 = -none- |successor1 = [[Satake Yoshitaka (1609–1672)|Satake Yoshitaka]] |birth_date = {{Birth date|1570|08|17}} |birth_place = [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]], Japan |death_date = {{Death date and age|1633|03|05|1570|08|17}} |death_place = [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Japan | father = [[Satake Yoshishige]] | spouse = Shōdō-in | commands = [[Kubota Domain]] | unit = [[Satake clan]] | allegiance = [[Satake clan]]
[[Toyotomi clan]]
[[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] | battles = [[:en:Battle of Hitotoribashi|Battle of Hitotoribashi]] (1586)
[[:en:Battle of Suriagehara|Battle of Suriagehara]] (1589)
[[Siege of Oshi]] (1590)
[[:en:Battle of Imafuku|Battle of Imafuku]] (1614) }} {{nihongo|'''Satake Yoshinobu'''|佐竹 義宣||August 17, 1570 – March 5, 1633}} was a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' in [[Sengoku period]] and early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] Japan under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and the 19th head of the [[Satake clan]] and 1st ''daimyō'' of [[Kubota Domain]] in [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]]. His [[:en:Courtesy title|Courtesy title]] was ''[[Kokushi (officials)|Sakon-no-e-shōshō]]'', later ''[[Daijō-kan|Ukyō-dayū]]'' and ''Jijū'' and his [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Court Rank]] was Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade.{{cite book |last1=Shichinomiya |first1=Keimi |title=常陸・秋田 佐竹一族 |date=2001 |publisher=Shinjinbutsu Oraisha |isbn=440402911X}} ==Biography== Yoshinobu was the eldest son of [[Satake Yoshishige]], and his mother was the daughter of [[:en:Date Harumune|Date Harumune]], which made him the cousin of [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]]. He was born at Ota Castle in [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]]. His early life was that of constant warfare. The Satake clan were fighting the Nasu clan to the north, and as a condition for peace, Yoshinobu was engaged to a daughter of the Nasu clan when he was only three years old. He participated in his first combat at [[:en:Battle of Hitotoribashi|Battle of Hitotoribashi]] in 1586, just before he turned 15. His father officially retired some time in 1589, turning the portion of clan chieftain over to Yoshinobu. During this period, the clan was threatened from the north by [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]] and from the south by [[:en:Hōjō Ujinao|Hōjō Ujinao]]; however, because he quickly pledged allegiance to [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] during the [[Siege of Odawara (1590)]], he was able to retain his landholdings. Under Hideyoshi's banner, he was counted among the six greatest generals of the [[Toyotomi clan]], together with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], [[Maeda Toshiie]], [[Shimazu Yoshihiro]], [[Mōri Terumoto]], and [[Uesugi Kagekatsu]]. During the siege of Odawara he was also able to extend his control to cover the over the whole of [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]] (with the exception of the territory of the [[Yūki clan]]), by defeating various supporters of the Hōjō, so that by the end of the campaign his ''[[kokudaka]]'' was Ranked at over 350,000 ''[[koku]]''. Yoshinobu enjoyed good relations with [[Ishida Mitsunari]], whom he has assisted at the [[Siege of Oshi]] in 1590. He relocated his seat to [[Mito Castle]], and contributed a large number of troops to Hideyoshi's subjugation of the [[Tohoku region]], and a much smaller number of troops to Hideyoshi's [[:en:Japanese invasions of Korea (1592–1598)|Korean invasion]], although he remained behind at [[Nagoya Castle (Hizen Province)|Nagoya Castle]] and later at [[:en:Fushimi Castle|Fushimi Castle]] with Hideyoshi and did not cross over to the continent. Under Hideyoshi, his ''kokudaka'' gradually was increased to 545,765 ''koku''. At the time of the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] the Satake clan was ordered by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] to participate in the campaign to destroy [[Uesugi Kagekatsu]] in [[:en:Aizu|Aizu]] and to provide a hostage. The Satake clan was divided, with [[Satake Yoshishige]] and many of the samurai strongly supporting Ieyasu and the Eastern Army, whereas Yoshinobu was hesitant, based on his long ties with Ishida Mitsunari and a secret treaty with the [[Uesugi clan]]. Instead of sending his army, he sent a small number of horsemen as reinforcements to [[Tokugawa Hidetada]]'s attack on the [[Sanada clan]], but for the most part remained in Mito attempting to stay neutral in the conflict. With the victory of the pro-Tokugawa forces at the Battle of Sekigahara and the subsequent establishment of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]], the Satake clan was punished by a reduction in their ''kokudaka'' to 180,000 ''koku'', and by being ordered to surrender their ancestral home territory in Hitachi Province for the new [[Kubota Domain]], in [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]].Saga, pp. 16–17. The Satake clan also was a direct descendant of the [[Minamoto clan]] and thus had a more legitimate hereditary claim to the title of ''shōgun'' than the [[Tokugawa clan]], which was another reason why Tokugawa Ieyasu viewed the clan with suspicion and as a potential threat. After arriving at their new domain in 1603, the Satake immediately had to face a [[Rokugō rebellion|rebellion]] of local ''[[rōnin]]'' loyal to [[Onodera Yoshimichi]], the former master of the region, which they defeated relatively quickly.{{sfnp|Turnbull|2010|pp=54, 55}} Subsequently, Yoshinobu took steps to gain favor with the new Tokugawa government by actively participating in the [[Siege of Osaka]], fighting Toyotomi commanders such as [[Kimura Shigenari]] and [[:en:Gotō Matabei|Gotō Matabei]] at the [[:en:Battle of Imafuku|Battle of Imafuku]]. Yoshinobu was married to Shōdō-in, the daughter of Nasu Suketane, to whom he had been engaged as a child. After her death, he remarried to a daughter of Tagaya Shigetsune of [[Shimotsuma Domain]]. He had no children by the first wife and both sons of the second marriage died in infancy. He also had a concubine, the daughter of Ashina Morioki. Yoshinobu adopted two sons as possible heirs. The eldest was the 5th son of Satake Yoshishige, Satake Yoshinao (1612-1656), who at the age of 14, fell asleep during a ''sarugaki'' performance at [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]], and was thus disinherited. he later became a monk. The younger was [[Satake Yoshitaka (1609–1672)|Satake Yoshitaka]], the son of Iwaki Sadataka of [[Kameda Domain]], who became his successor. Yoshinobu died at the domain's Edo residence in [[Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda]] in 1633. ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} ==Bibliography== * Saga, Jun'ichi (1987). ''Memories of Silk and Straw: A Self-Portrait of Small-Town Japan''. New York: Kodansha International. *{{cite book|last= Turnbull|first= Stephen|author-link= Stephen Turnbull (historian)|title= Hatamoto. Samurai Horse and Foot Guards 1540–1724|date=2010|publisher= [[Osprey Publishing]]|location= [[Oxford]]|isbn= 978-184603-478-7}} ==Further reading== {{People of the Sengoku period|state=autocollapse}} {{Authority control}}]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Tozama daimyo]] [[Category:1570 births]] [[Category:1633 deaths]] [[Category:Satake clan]] [[Category:Toyotomi retainers]] [[Category:People of Sengoku-period Japan]] [[Category:People of Edo-period Japan]] }} [[file:Satake.jpg|40px]] [[sn:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[en:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] {{Commons category|Satake Yoshinobu}} [[el:Σατάκε Γιοσινόμπου]] [[es:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[fa:ساتاکه یوشینوبو]] [[fr:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[it:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ja:佐竹義宣 (右京大夫)]] [[ko:사타케 요시노부 (1570년)]] [[pt:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ro:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ru:Сатакэ Ёсинобу]] [[tr:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[zh:佐竹義宣]]

The younger was [[Satake Yoshitaka (1609–1672)|Satake Yoshitaka]], the son of Iwaki Sadataka of [[Kameda Domain]], who became his successor. Yoshinobu died at the domain's Edo residence in [[Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda]] in 1633. ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} ==Bibliography== * Saga, Jun'ichi (1987). ''Memories of Silk and Straw: A Self-Portrait of Small-Town Japan''. New York: Kodansha International. *{{cite book|last= Turnbull|first= Stephen|author-link= Stephen Turnbull (historian)|title= Hatamoto. {{short description|Japanese daimyō (1570–1633)}} {{family name hatnote| Satake|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox officeholder |honorific_prefix= |name = Satake Yoshinobu |native_name =佐竹 義宣
|image = Satake Yoshinobu.jpg |caption =Satake Yoshinobu, portrait at Tentoku-ji, Akita | monarch1 = '''Shōgun''' {{plainlist| *[[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] *[[Tokugawa Hidetada]] *[[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] }} | office = Head of [[Satake clan]] | termstart = 1589 | termend = 1633 | predecessor = [[Satake Yoshishige]] | successor = [[Satake Yoshitaka (1609–1672)|Satake Yoshitaka]] |office1= 1st ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[Kubota Domain]] |term_start1 = 1602 |term_end1 = 1633 |predecessor1 = -none- |successor1 = [[Satake Yoshitaka (1609–1672)|Satake Yoshitaka]] |birth_date = {{Birth date|1570|08|17}} |birth_place = [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]], Japan |death_date = {{Death date and age|1633|03|05|1570|08|17}} |death_place = [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Japan | father = [[Satake Yoshishige]] | spouse = Shōdō-in | commands = [[Kubota Domain]] | unit = [[Satake clan]] | allegiance = [[Satake clan]]
[[Toyotomi clan]]
[[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] | battles = [[:en:Battle of Hitotoribashi|Battle of Hitotoribashi]] (1586)
[[:en:Battle of Suriagehara|Battle of Suriagehara]] (1589)
[[Siege of Oshi]] (1590)
[[:en:Battle of Imafuku|Battle of Imafuku]] (1614) }} {{nihongo|'''Satake Yoshinobu'''|佐竹 義宣||August 17, 1570 – March 5, 1633}} was a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' in [[Sengoku period]] and early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] Japan under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and the 19th head of the [[Satake clan]] and 1st ''daimyō'' of [[Kubota Domain]] in [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]]. His [[:en:Courtesy title|Courtesy title]] was ''[[Kokushi (officials)|Sakon-no-e-shōshō]]'', later ''[[Daijō-kan|Ukyō-dayū]]'' and ''Jijū'' and his [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Court Rank]] was Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade.{{cite book |last1=Shichinomiya |first1=Keimi |title=常陸・秋田 佐竹一族 |date=2001 |publisher=Shinjinbutsu Oraisha |isbn=440402911X}} ==Biography== Yoshinobu was the eldest son of [[Satake Yoshishige]], and his mother was the daughter of [[:en:Date Harumune|Date Harumune]], which made him the cousin of [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]]. He was born at Ota Castle in [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]]. His early life was that of constant warfare. The Satake clan were fighting the Nasu clan to the north, and as a condition for peace, Yoshinobu was engaged to a daughter of the Nasu clan when he was only three years old. He participated in his first combat at [[:en:Battle of Hitotoribashi|Battle of Hitotoribashi]] in 1586, just before he turned 15. His father officially retired some time in 1589, turning the portion of clan chieftain over to Yoshinobu. During this period, the clan was threatened from the north by [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]] and from the south by [[:en:Hōjō Ujinao|Hōjō Ujinao]]; however, because he quickly pledged allegiance to [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] during the [[Siege of Odawara (1590)]], he was able to retain his landholdings. Under Hideyoshi's banner, he was counted among the six greatest generals of the [[Toyotomi clan]], together with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], [[Maeda Toshiie]], [[Shimazu Yoshihiro]], [[Mōri Terumoto]], and [[Uesugi Kagekatsu]]. During the siege of Odawara he was also able to extend his control to cover the over the whole of [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]] (with the exception of the territory of the [[Yūki clan]]), by defeating various supporters of the Hōjō, so that by the end of the campaign his ''[[kokudaka]]'' was Ranked at over 350,000 ''[[koku]]''. Yoshinobu enjoyed good relations with [[Ishida Mitsunari]], whom he has assisted at the [[Siege of Oshi]] in 1590. He relocated his seat to [[Mito Castle]], and contributed a large number of troops to Hideyoshi's subjugation of the [[Tohoku region]], and a much smaller number of troops to Hideyoshi's [[:en:Japanese invasions of Korea (1592–1598)|Korean invasion]], although he remained behind at [[Nagoya Castle (Hizen Province)|Nagoya Castle]] and later at [[:en:Fushimi Castle|Fushimi Castle]] with Hideyoshi and did not cross over to the continent. Under Hideyoshi, his ''kokudaka'' gradually was increased to 545,765 ''koku''. At the time of the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] the Satake clan was ordered by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] to participate in the campaign to destroy [[Uesugi Kagekatsu]] in [[:en:Aizu|Aizu]] and to provide a hostage. The Satake clan was divided, with [[Satake Yoshishige]] and many of the samurai strongly supporting Ieyasu and the Eastern Army, whereas Yoshinobu was hesitant, based on his long ties with Ishida Mitsunari and a secret treaty with the [[Uesugi clan]]. Instead of sending his army, he sent a small number of horsemen as reinforcements to [[Tokugawa Hidetada]]'s attack on the [[Sanada clan]], but for the most part remained in Mito attempting to stay neutral in the conflict. With the victory of the pro-Tokugawa forces at the Battle of Sekigahara and the subsequent establishment of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]], the Satake clan was punished by a reduction in their ''kokudaka'' to 180,000 ''koku'', and by being ordered to surrender their ancestral home territory in Hitachi Province for the new [[Kubota Domain]], in [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]].Saga, pp. 16–17. The Satake clan also was a direct descendant of the [[Minamoto clan]] and thus had a more legitimate hereditary claim to the title of ''shōgun'' than the [[Tokugawa clan]], which was another reason why Tokugawa Ieyasu viewed the clan with suspicion and as a potential threat. After arriving at their new domain in 1603, the Satake immediately had to face a [[Rokugō rebellion|rebellion]] of local ''[[rōnin]]'' loyal to [[Onodera Yoshimichi]], the former master of the region, which they defeated relatively quickly.{{sfnp|Turnbull|2010|pp=54, 55}} Subsequently, Yoshinobu took steps to gain favor with the new Tokugawa government by actively participating in the [[Siege of Osaka]], fighting Toyotomi commanders such as [[Kimura Shigenari]] and [[:en:Gotō Matabei|Gotō Matabei]] at the [[:en:Battle of Imafuku|Battle of Imafuku]]. Yoshinobu was married to Shōdō-in, the daughter of Nasu Suketane, to whom he had been engaged as a child. After her death, he remarried to a daughter of Tagaya Shigetsune of [[Shimotsuma Domain]]. He had no children by the first wife and both sons of the second marriage died in infancy. He also had a concubine, the daughter of Ashina Morioki. Yoshinobu adopted two sons as possible heirs. The eldest was the 5th son of Satake Yoshishige, Satake Yoshinao (1612-1656), who at the age of 14, fell asleep during a ''sarugaki'' performance at [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]], and was thus disinherited. he later became a monk. The younger was [[Satake Yoshitaka (1609–1672)|Satake Yoshitaka]], the son of Iwaki Sadataka of [[Kameda Domain]], who became his successor. Yoshinobu died at the domain's Edo residence in [[Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda]] in 1633. ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} ==Bibliography== * Saga, Jun'ichi (1987). ''Memories of Silk and Straw: A Self-Portrait of Small-Town Japan''. New York: Kodansha International. *{{cite book|last= Turnbull|first= Stephen|author-link= Stephen Turnbull (historian)|title= Hatamoto. Samurai Horse and Foot Guards 1540–1724|date=2010|publisher= [[Osprey Publishing]]|location= [[Oxford]]|isbn= 978-184603-478-7}} ==Further reading== {{People of the Sengoku period|state=autocollapse}} {{Authority control}}]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Tozama daimyo]] [[Category:1633 deaths]] [[Category:Satake clan]] [[Category:Toyotomi retainers]] [[Category:People of Sengoku-period Japan]] [[Category:People of Edo-period Japan]] }} [[file:Satake.jpg|40px]] [[sn:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[en:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] {{Commons category|Satake Yoshinobu}} [[el:Σατάκε Γιοσινόμπου]] [[es:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[fa:ساتاکه یوشینوبو]] [[fr:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[it:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ja:佐竹義宣 (右京大夫)]] [[ko:사타케 요시노부 (1570년)]] [[pt:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ro:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ru:Сатакэ Ёсинобу]] [[tr:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[zh:佐竹義宣]]

After arriving at their new domain in 1603, the Satake immediately had to face a [[Rokugō rebellion|rebellion]] of local ''[[rōnin]]'' loyal to [[Onodera Yoshimichi]], the former master of the region, which they defeated relatively quickly.{{sfnp|Turnbull|2010|pp=54, 55}} Subsequently, Yoshinobu took steps to gain favor with the new Tokugawa government by actively participating in the [[Siege of Osaka]], fighting Toyotomi commanders such as [[Kimura Shigenari]] and [[:en:Gotō Matabei|Gotō Matabei]] at the [[:en:Battle of Imafuku|Battle of Imafuku]]. Yoshinobu was married to Shōdō-in, the daughter of Nasu Suketane, to whom he had been engaged as a child. After her death, he remarried to a daughter of Tagaya Shigetsune of [[Shimotsuma Domain]]. He had no children by the first wife and both sons of the second marriage died in infancy. He also had a concubine, the daughter of Ashina Morioki. Yoshinobu adopted two sons as possible heirs. The eldest was the 5th son of Satake Yoshishige, Satake Yoshinao (1612-1656), who at the age of 14, fell asleep during a ''sarugaki'' performance at [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]], and was thus disinherited. he later became a monk. The younger was [[Satake Yoshitaka (1609–1672)|Satake Yoshitaka]], the son of Iwaki Sadataka of [[:en:Kameda Domain|Kameda Domain]], who became his successor. Yoshinobu died at the domain's Edo residence in [[Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda]] in 1633. ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} ==Bibliography== * Saga, Jun'ichi (1987). ''Memories of Silk and Straw: A Self-Portrait of Small-Town Japan''. New York: Kodansha International. *{{cite book|last= Turnbull|first= Stephen|author-link= Stephen Turnbull (historian)|title= Hatamoto. Samurai Horse and Foot Guards 1540–1724|date=2010|publisher= [[Osprey Publishing]]|location= [[Oxford]]|isbn= 978-184603-478-7}} ==Further reading== {{People of the Sengoku period|state=autocollapse}} {{Authority control}}]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Tozama daimyo]] [[Category:1570 births]] [[Category:1633 deaths]] [[Category:Satake clan]] [[Category:Toyotomi retainers]] [[Category:People of Sengoku-period Japan]] [[Category:People of Edo-period Japan]] }} [[file:Satake.jpg|40px]] [[sn:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[en:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] {{Commons category|Satake Yoshinobu}} [[el:Σατάκε Γιοσινόμπου]] [[es:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[fa:ساتاکه یوشینوبو]] [[fr:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[it:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ja:佐竹義宣 (右京大夫)]] [[ko:사타케 요시노부 (1570년)]] [[pt:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ro:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ru:Сатакэ Ёсинобу]] [[tr:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[zh:佐竹義宣]]

{{short description|Japanese daimyō (1570–1633)}} {{family name hatnote| Satake|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox officeholder |honorific_prefix= |name = Satake Yoshinobu |native_name =佐竹 義宣
|image = Satake Yoshinobu.jpg |caption =Satake Yoshinobu, portrait at Tentoku-ji, Akita | monarch1 = '''Shōgun''' {{plainlist| *[[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] *[[Tokugawa Hidetada]] *[[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] }} | office = Head of [[Satake clan]] | termstart = 1589 | termend = 1633 | predecessor = [[Satake Yoshishige]] | successor = [[Satake Yoshitaka (1609–1672)|Satake Yoshitaka]] |office1= 1st ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[Kubota Domain]] |term_start1 = 1602 |term_end1 = 1633 |predecessor1 = -none- |successor1 = [[Satake Yoshitaka (1609–1672)|Satake Yoshitaka]] |birth_date = {{Birth date|1570|08|17}} |birth_place = [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]], Japan |death_date = {{Death date and age|1633|03|05|1570|08|17}} |death_place = [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Japan | father = [[Satake Yoshishige]] | spouse = Shōdō-in | commands = [[Kubota Domain]] | unit = [[Satake clan]] | allegiance = [[Satake clan]]
[[Toyotomi clan]]
[[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] | battles = [[:en:Battle of Hitotoribashi|Battle of Hitotoribashi]] (1586)
[[:en:Battle of Suriagehara|Battle of Suriagehara]] (1589)
[[Siege of Oshi]] (1590)
[[:en:Battle of Imafuku|Battle of Imafuku]] (1614) }} {{nihongo|'''Satake Yoshinobu'''|佐竹 義宣||August 17, 1570 – March 5, 1633}} was a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' in [[Sengoku period]] and early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] Japan under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and the 19th head of the [[Satake clan]] and 1st ''daimyō'' of [[Kubota Domain]] in [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]]. His [[:en:Courtesy title|Courtesy title]] was ''[[Kokushi (officials)|Sakon-no-e-shōshō]]'', later ''[[Daijō-kan|Ukyō-dayū]]'' and ''Jijū'' and his [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Court Rank]] was Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade.{{cite book |last1=Shichinomiya |first1=Keimi |title=常陸・秋田 佐竹一族 |date=2001 |publisher=Shinjinbutsu Oraisha |isbn=440402911X}} ==Biography== Yoshinobu was the eldest son of [[Satake Yoshishige]], and his mother was the daughter of [[:en:Date Harumune|Date Harumune]], which made him the cousin of [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]]. He was born at Ota Castle in [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]]. His early life was that of constant warfare. The Satake clan were fighting the Nasu clan to the north, and as a condition for peace, Yoshinobu was engaged to a daughter of the Nasu clan when he was only three years old. He participated in his first combat at [[:en:Battle of Hitotoribashi|Battle of Hitotoribashi]] in 1586, just before he turned 15. His father officially retired some time in 1589, turning the portion of clan chieftain over to Yoshinobu. During this period, the clan was threatened from the north by [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]] and from the south by [[:en:Hōjō Ujinao|Hōjō Ujinao]]; however, because he quickly pledged allegiance to [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] during the [[Siege of Odawara (1590)]], he was able to retain his landholdings. Under Hideyoshi's banner, he was counted among the six greatest generals of the [[Toyotomi clan]], together with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], [[Maeda Toshiie]], [[Shimazu Yoshihiro]], [[Mōri Terumoto]], and [[Uesugi Kagekatsu]]. During the siege of Odawara he was also able to extend his control to cover the over the whole of [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]] (with the exception of the territory of the [[Yūki clan]]), by defeating various supporters of the Hōjō, so that by the end of the campaign his ''[[kokudaka]]'' was Ranked at over 350,000 ''[[koku]]''. Yoshinobu enjoyed good relations with [[Ishida Mitsunari]], whom he has assisted at the [[Siege of Oshi]] in 1590. He relocated his seat to [[Mito Castle]], and contributed a large number of troops to Hideyoshi's subjugation of the [[Tohoku region]], and a much smaller number of troops to Hideyoshi's [[:en:Japanese invasions of Korea (1592–1598)|Korean invasion]], although he remained behind at [[Nagoya Castle (Hizen Province)|Nagoya Castle]] and later at [[:en:Fushimi Castle|Fushimi Castle]] with Hideyoshi and did not cross over to the continent. Under Hideyoshi, his ''kokudaka'' gradually was increased to 545,765 ''koku''. At the time of the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] the Satake clan was ordered by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] to participate in the campaign to destroy [[Uesugi Kagekatsu]] in [[:en:Aizu|Aizu]] and to provide a hostage. The Satake clan was divided, with [[Satake Yoshishige]] and many of the samurai strongly supporting Ieyasu and the Eastern Army, whereas Yoshinobu was hesitant, based on his long ties with Ishida Mitsunari and a secret treaty with the [[Uesugi clan]]. Instead of sending his army, he sent a small number of horsemen as reinforcements to [[Tokugawa Hidetada]]'s attack on the [[Sanada clan]], but for the most part remained in Mito attempting to stay neutral in the conflict. With the victory of the pro-Tokugawa forces at the Battle of Sekigahara and the subsequent establishment of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]], the Satake clan was punished by a reduction in their ''kokudaka'' to 180,000 ''koku'', and by being ordered to surrender their ancestral home territory in Hitachi Province for the new [[Kubota Domain]], in [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]].Saga, pp. 16–17. The Satake clan also was a direct descendant of the [[Minamoto clan]] and thus had a more legitimate hereditary claim to the title of ''shōgun'' than the [[Tokugawa clan]], which was another reason why Tokugawa Ieyasu viewed the clan with suspicion and as a potential threat. After arriving at their new domain in 1603, the Satake immediately had to face a [[Rokugō rebellion|rebellion]] of local ''[[rōnin]]'' loyal to [[Onodera Yoshimichi]], the former master of the region, which they defeated relatively quickly.{{sfnp|Turnbull|2010|pp=54, 55}} Subsequently, Yoshinobu took steps to gain favor with the new Tokugawa government by actively participating in the [[Siege of Osaka]], fighting Toyotomi commanders such as [[Kimura Shigenari]] and [[:en:Gotō Matabei|Gotō Matabei]] at the [[:en:Battle of Imafuku|Battle of Imafuku]]. Yoshinobu was married to Shōdō-in, the daughter of Nasu Suketane, to whom he had been engaged as a child. After her death, he remarried to a daughter of Tagaya Shigetsune of [[Shimotsuma Domain]]. He had no children by the first wife and both sons of the second marriage died in infancy. He also had a concubine, the daughter of Ashina Morioki. Yoshinobu adopted two sons as possible heirs. The eldest was the 5th son of Satake Yoshishige, Satake Yoshinao (1612-1656), who at the age of 14, fell asleep during a ''sarugaki'' performance at [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]], and was thus disinherited. he later became a monk. The younger was [[Satake Yoshitaka (1609–1672)|Satake Yoshitaka]], the son of Iwaki Sadataka of [[:en:Kameda Domain|Kameda Domain]], who became his successor. Yoshinobu died at the domain's Edo residence in [[:en:Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda]] in 1633. ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} ==Bibliography== * Saga, Jun'ichi (1987). ''Memories of Silk and Straw: A Self-Portrait of Small-Town Japan''. New York: Kodansha International. *{{cite book|last= Turnbull|first= Stephen|author-link= Stephen Turnbull (historian)|title= Hatamoto. Samurai Horse and Foot Guards 1540–1724|date=2010|publisher= [[Osprey Publishing]]|location= [[Oxford]]|isbn= 978-184603-478-7}} ==Further reading== {{People of the Sengoku period|state=autocollapse}} {{Authority control}}]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Tozama daimyo]] [[Category:1570 births]] [[Category:1633 deaths]] [[Category:Satake clan]] [[Category:Toyotomi retainers]] [[Category:People of Sengoku-period Japan]] [[Category:People of Edo-period Japan]] }} [[file:Satake.jpg|40px]] [[sn:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[en:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] {{Commons category|Satake Yoshinobu}} [[el:Σατάκε Γιοσινόμπου]] [[es:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[fa:ساتاکه یوشینوبو]] [[fr:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[it:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ja:佐竹義宣 (右京大夫)]] [[ko:사타케 요시노부 (1570년)]] [[pt:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ro:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ru:Сатакэ Ёсинобу]] [[tr:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[zh:佐竹義宣]]

At the time of the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] the Satake clan was ordered by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] to participate in the campaign to destroy [[Uesugi Kagekatsu]] in [[:en:Aizu|Aizu]] and to provide a hostage. The Satake clan was divided, with [[Satake Yoshishige]] and many of the samurai strongly supporting Ieyasu and the Eastern Army, whereas Yoshinobu was hesitant, based on his long ties with Ishida Mitsunari and a secret treaty with the [[Uesugi clan]]. Instead of sending his army, he sent a small number of horsemen as reinforcements to [[Tokugawa Hidetada]]'s attack on the [[Sanada clan]], but for the most part remained in Mito attempting to stay neutral in the conflict. With the victory of the pro-Tokugawa forces at the Battle of Sekigahara and the subsequent establishment of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]], the Satake clan was punished by a reduction in their ''kokudaka'' to 180,000 ''koku'', and by being ordered to surrender their ancestral home territory in Hitachi Province for the new [[Kubota Domain]], in [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]].Saga, pp. 16–17. The Satake clan also was a direct descendant of the [[Minamoto clan]] and thus had a more legitimate hereditary claim to the title of ''shōgun'' than the [[Tokugawa clan]], which was another reason why Tokugawa Ieyasu viewed the clan with suspicion and as a potential threat. After arriving at their new domain in 1603, the Satake immediately had to face a [[Rokugō rebellion|rebellion]] of local ''[[rōnin]]'' loyal to [[Onodera Yoshimichi]], the former master of the region, which they defeated relatively quickly.{{sfnp|Turnbull|2010|pp=54, 55}} Subsequently, Yoshinobu took steps to gain favor with the new Tokugawa government by actively participating in the [[Siege of Osaka]], fighting Toyotomi commanders such as [[:en:Kimura Shigenari|Kimura Shigenari]] and [[:en:Gotō Matabei|Gotō Matabei]] at the [[:en:Battle of Imafuku|Battle of Imafuku]]. Yoshinobu was married to Shōdō-in, the daughter of Nasu Suketane, to whom he had been engaged as a child. After her death, he remarried to a daughter of Tagaya Shigetsune of [[Shimotsuma Domain]]. He had no children by the first wife and both sons of the second marriage died in infancy. He also had a concubine, the daughter of Ashina Morioki. Yoshinobu adopted two sons as possible heirs. The eldest was the 5th son of Satake Yoshishige, Satake Yoshinao (1612-1656), who at the age of 14, fell asleep during a ''sarugaki'' performance at [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]], and was thus disinherited. he later became a monk. The younger was [[Satake Yoshitaka (1609–1672)|Satake Yoshitaka]], the son of Iwaki Sadataka of [[:en:Kameda Domain|Kameda Domain]], who became his successor. Yoshinobu died at the domain's Edo residence in [[:en:Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda]] in 1633. ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} ==Bibliography== * Saga, Jun'ichi (1987). ''Memories of Silk and Straw: A Self-Portrait of Small-Town Japan''. New York: Kodansha International. *{{cite book|last= Turnbull|first= Stephen|author-link= Stephen Turnbull (historian)|title= Hatamoto. Samurai Horse and Foot Guards 1540–1724|date=2010|publisher= [[Osprey Publishing]]|location= [[Oxford]]|isbn= 978-184603-478-7}} ==Further reading== {{People of the Sengoku period|state=autocollapse}} {{Authority control}}]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Tozama daimyo]] [[Category:1570 births]] [[Category:1633 deaths]] [[Category:Satake clan]] [[Category:Toyotomi retainers]] [[Category:People of Sengoku-period Japan]] [[Category:People of Edo-period Japan]] }} [[file:Satake.jpg|40px]] [[sn:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[en:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] {{Commons category|Satake Yoshinobu}} [[el:Σατάκε Γιοσινόμπου]] [[es:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[fa:ساتاکه یوشینوبو]] [[fr:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[it:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ja:佐竹義宣 (右京大夫)]] [[ko:사타케 요시노부 (1570년)]] [[pt:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ro:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ru:Сатакэ Ёсинобу]] [[tr:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[zh:佐竹義宣]]

[[:en:Battle of Imafuku|Battle of Imafuku]] (1614) }} {{nihongo|'''Satake Yoshinobu'''|佐竹 義宣||August 17, 1570 – March 5, 1633}} was a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' in [[Sengoku period]] and early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] Japan under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and the 19th head of the [[Satake clan]] and 1st ''daimyō'' of [[Kubota Domain]] in [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]]. His [[:en:Courtesy title|Courtesy title]] was ''[[Kokushi (officials)|Sakon-no-e-shōshō]]'', later ''[[Daijō-kan|Ukyō-dayū]]'' and ''Jijū'' and his [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Court Rank]] was Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade.{{cite book |last1=Shichinomiya |first1=Keimi |title=常陸・秋田 佐竹一族 |date=2001 |publisher=Shinjinbutsu Oraisha |isbn=440402911X}} ==Biography== Yoshinobu was the eldest son of [[Satake Yoshishige]], and his mother was the daughter of [[:en:Date Harumune|Date Harumune]], which made him the cousin of [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]]. He was born at Ota Castle in [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]]. His early life was that of constant warfare. The Satake clan were fighting the Nasu clan to the north, and as a condition for peace, Yoshinobu was engaged to a daughter of the Nasu clan when he was only three years old. He participated in his first combat at [[:en:Battle of Hitotoribashi|Battle of Hitotoribashi]] in 1586, just before he turned 15. His father officially retired some time in 1589, turning the portion of clan chieftain over to Yoshinobu. During this period, the clan was threatened from the north by [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]] and from the south by [[:en:Hōjō Ujinao|Hōjō Ujinao]]; however, because he quickly pledged allegiance to [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] during the [[Siege of Odawara (1590)]], he was able to retain his landholdings. Under Hideyoshi's banner, he was counted among the six greatest generals of the [[Toyotomi clan]], together with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], [[Maeda Toshiie]], [[Shimazu Yoshihiro]], [[Mōri Terumoto]], and [[Uesugi Kagekatsu]]. During the siege of Odawara he was also able to extend his control to cover the over the whole of [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]] (with the exception of the territory of the [[Yūki clan]]), by defeating various supporters of the Hōjō, so that by the end of the campaign his ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' was Ranked at over 350,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''. Yoshinobu enjoyed good relations with [[Ishida Mitsunari]], whom he has assisted at the [[Siege of Oshi]] in 1590. He relocated his seat to [[Mito Castle]], and contributed a large number of troops to Hideyoshi's subjugation of the [[Tohoku region]], and a much smaller number of troops to Hideyoshi's [[:en:Japanese invasions of Korea (1592–1598)|Korean invasion]], although he remained behind at [[Nagoya Castle (Hizen Province)|Nagoya Castle]] and later at [[:en:Fushimi Castle|Fushimi Castle]] with Hideyoshi and did not cross over to the continent. Under Hideyoshi, his ''kokudaka'' gradually was increased to 545,765 ''koku''. At the time of the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] the Satake clan was ordered by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] to participate in the campaign to destroy [[Uesugi Kagekatsu]] in [[:en:Aizu|Aizu]] and to provide a hostage. The Satake clan was divided, with [[Satake Yoshishige]] and many of the samurai strongly supporting Ieyasu and the Eastern Army, whereas Yoshinobu was hesitant, based on his long ties with Ishida Mitsunari and a secret treaty with the [[Uesugi clan]]. Instead of sending his army, he sent a small number of horsemen as reinforcements to [[Tokugawa Hidetada]]'s attack on the [[Sanada clan]], but for the most part remained in Mito attempting to stay neutral in the conflict. With the victory of the pro-Tokugawa forces at the Battle of Sekigahara and the subsequent establishment of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]], the Satake clan was punished by a reduction in their ''kokudaka'' to 180,000 ''koku'', and by being ordered to surrender their ancestral home territory in Hitachi Province for the new [[Kubota Domain]], in [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]].Saga, pp. 16–17. The Satake clan also was a direct descendant of the [[Minamoto clan]] and thus had a more legitimate hereditary claim to the title of ''shōgun'' than the [[Tokugawa clan]], which was another reason why Tokugawa Ieyasu viewed the clan with suspicion and as a potential threat. After arriving at their new domain in 1603, the Satake immediately had to face a [[Rokugō rebellion|rebellion]] of local ''[[rōnin]]'' loyal to [[Onodera Yoshimichi]], the former master of the region, which they defeated relatively quickly.{{sfnp|Turnbull|2010|pp=54, 55}} Subsequently, Yoshinobu took steps to gain favor with the new Tokugawa government by actively participating in the [[Siege of Osaka]], fighting Toyotomi commanders such as [[:en:Kimura Shigenari|Kimura Shigenari]] and [[:en:Gotō Matabei|Gotō Matabei]] at the [[:en:Battle of Imafuku|Battle of Imafuku]]. Yoshinobu was married to Shōdō-in, the daughter of Nasu Suketane, to whom he had been engaged as a child. After her death, he remarried to a daughter of Tagaya Shigetsune of [[Shimotsuma Domain]]. He had no children by the first wife and both sons of the second marriage died in infancy. He also had a concubine, the daughter of Ashina Morioki. Yoshinobu adopted two sons as possible heirs. The eldest was the 5th son of Satake Yoshishige, Satake Yoshinao (1612-1656), who at the age of 14, fell asleep during a ''sarugaki'' performance at [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]], and was thus disinherited. he later became a monk. The younger was [[Satake Yoshitaka (1609–1672)|Satake Yoshitaka]], the son of Iwaki Sadataka of [[:en:Kameda Domain|Kameda Domain]], who became his successor. Yoshinobu died at the domain's Edo residence in [[:en:Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda]] in 1633. ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} ==Bibliography== * Saga, Jun'ichi (1987). ''Memories of Silk and Straw: A Self-Portrait of Small-Town Japan''. New York: Kodansha International. *{{cite book|last= Turnbull|first= Stephen|author-link= Stephen Turnbull (historian)|title= Hatamoto. Samurai Horse and Foot Guards 1540–1724|date=2010|publisher= [[Osprey Publishing]]|location= [[Oxford]]|isbn= 978-184603-478-7}} ==Further reading== {{People of the Sengoku period|state=autocollapse}} {{Authority control}}]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Tozama daimyo]] [[Category:1570 births]] [[Category:1633 deaths]] [[Category:Satake clan]] [[Category:Toyotomi retainers]] [[Category:People of Sengoku-period Japan]] [[Category:People of Edo-period Japan]] }} [[file:Satake.jpg|40px]] [[sn:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[en:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] {{Commons category|Satake Yoshinobu}} [[el:Σατάκε Γιοσινόμπου]] [[es:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[fa:ساتاکه یوشینوبو]] [[fr:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[it:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ja:佐竹義宣 (右京大夫)]] [[ko:사타케 요시노부 (1570년)]] [[pt:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ro:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ru:Сатакэ Ёсинобу]] [[tr:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[zh:佐竹義宣]]

Under Hideyoshi's banner, he was counted among the six greatest generals of the [[Toyotomi clan]], together with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], [[Maeda Toshiie]], [[Shimazu Yoshihiro]], [[Mōri Terumoto]], and [[Uesugi Kagekatsu]]. During the siege of Odawara he was also able to extend his control to cover the over the whole of [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]] (with the exception of the territory of the [[Yūki clan]]), by defeating various supporters of the Hōjō, so that by the end of the campaign his ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' was Ranked at over 350,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''. Yoshinobu enjoyed good relations with [[Ishida Mitsunari]], whom he has assisted at the [[Siege of Oshi]] in 1590. He relocated his seat to [[Mito Castle]], and contributed a large number of troops to Hideyoshi's subjugation of the [[Tohoku region]], and a much smaller number of troops to Hideyoshi's [[:en:Japanese invasions of Korea (1592–1598)|Korean invasion]], although he remained behind at [[Nagoya Castle (Hizen Province)|Nagoya Castle]] and later at [[:en:Fushimi Castle|Fushimi Castle]] with Hideyoshi and did not cross over to the continent. Under Hideyoshi, his ''kokudaka'' gradually was increased to 545,765 ''koku''. At the time of the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] the Satake clan was ordered by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] to participate in the campaign to destroy [[Uesugi Kagekatsu]] in [[:en:Aizu|Aizu]] and to provide a hostage. The Satake clan was divided, with [[Satake Yoshishige]] and many of the samurai strongly supporting Ieyasu and the Eastern Army, whereas Yoshinobu was hesitant, based on his long ties with Ishida Mitsunari and a secret treaty with the [[Uesugi clan]]. Instead of sending his army, he sent a small number of horsemen as reinforcements to [[Tokugawa Hidetada]]'s attack on the [[Sanada clan]], but for the most part remained in Mito attempting to stay neutral in the conflict. With the victory of the pro-Tokugawa forces at the Battle of Sekigahara and the subsequent establishment of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]], the Satake clan was punished by a reduction in their ''kokudaka'' to 180,000 ''koku'', and by being ordered to surrender their ancestral home territory in Hitachi Province for the new [[:en:Kubota Domain|Kubota Domain]], in [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]].Saga, pp. 16–17. The Satake clan also was a direct descendant of the [[Minamoto clan]] and thus had a more legitimate hereditary claim to the title of ''shōgun'' than the [[Tokugawa clan]], which was another reason why Tokugawa Ieyasu viewed the clan with suspicion and as a potential threat. After arriving at their new domain in 1603, the Satake immediately had to face a [[Rokugō rebellion|rebellion]] of local ''[[rōnin]]'' loyal to [[Onodera Yoshimichi]], the former master of the region, which they defeated relatively quickly.{{sfnp|Turnbull|2010|pp=54, 55}} Subsequently, Yoshinobu took steps to gain favor with the new Tokugawa government by actively participating in the [[Siege of Osaka]], fighting Toyotomi commanders such as [[:en:Kimura Shigenari|Kimura Shigenari]] and [[:en:Gotō Matabei|Gotō Matabei]] at the [[:en:Battle of Imafuku|Battle of Imafuku]]. Yoshinobu was married to Shōdō-in, the daughter of Nasu Suketane, to whom he had been engaged as a child. After her death, he remarried to a daughter of Tagaya Shigetsune of [[Shimotsuma Domain]]. He had no children by the first wife and both sons of the second marriage died in infancy. He also had a concubine, the daughter of Ashina Morioki. Yoshinobu adopted two sons as possible heirs. The eldest was the 5th son of Satake Yoshishige, Satake Yoshinao (1612-1656), who at the age of 14, fell asleep during a ''sarugaki'' performance at [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]], and was thus disinherited. he later became a monk. The younger was [[Satake Yoshitaka (1609–1672)|Satake Yoshitaka]], the son of Iwaki Sadataka of [[:en:Kameda Domain|Kameda Domain]], who became his successor. Yoshinobu died at the domain's Edo residence in [[:en:Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda]] in 1633. ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} ==Bibliography== * Saga, Jun'ichi (1987). ''Memories of Silk and Straw: A Self-Portrait of Small-Town Japan''. New York: Kodansha International. *{{cite book|last= Turnbull|first= Stephen|author-link= Stephen Turnbull (historian)|title= Hatamoto. Samurai Horse and Foot Guards 1540–1724|date=2010|publisher= [[Osprey Publishing]]|location= [[Oxford]]|isbn= 978-184603-478-7}} ==Further reading== {{People of the Sengoku period|state=autocollapse}} {{Authority control}}]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Tozama daimyo]] [[Category:1570 births]] [[Category:1633 deaths]] [[Category:Satake clan]] [[Category:Toyotomi retainers]] [[Category:People of Sengoku-period Japan]] [[Category:People of Edo-period Japan]] }} [[file:Satake.jpg|40px]] [[sn:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[en:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] {{Commons category|Satake Yoshinobu}} [[el:Σατάκε Γιοσινόμπου]] [[es:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[fa:ساتاکه یوشینوبو]] [[fr:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[it:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ja:佐竹義宣 (右京大夫)]] [[ko:사타케 요시노부 (1570년)]] [[pt:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ro:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ru:Сатакэ Ёсинобу]] [[tr:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[zh:佐竹義宣]]

[[:en:Battle of Imafuku|Battle of Imafuku]] (1614) }} {{nihongo|'''Satake Yoshinobu'''|佐竹 義宣||August 17, 1570 – March 5, 1633}} was a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' in [[Sengoku period]] and early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] Japan under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and the 19th head of the [[Satake clan]] and 1st ''daimyō'' of [[:en:Kubota Domain|Kubota Domain]] in [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]]. His [[:en:Courtesy title|Courtesy title]] was ''[[:en:Kokushi (officials)|Sakon-no-e-shōshō]]'', later ''[[Daijō-kan|Ukyō-dayū]]'' and ''Jijū'' and his [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Court Rank]] was Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade.{{cite book |last1=Shichinomiya |first1=Keimi |title=常陸・秋田 佐竹一族 |date=2001 |publisher=Shinjinbutsu Oraisha |isbn=440402911X}} ==Biography== Yoshinobu was the eldest son of [[Satake Yoshishige]], and his mother was the daughter of [[:en:Date Harumune|Date Harumune]], which made him the cousin of [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]]. He was born at Ota Castle in [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]]. His early life was that of constant warfare. The Satake clan were fighting the Nasu clan to the north, and as a condition for peace, Yoshinobu was engaged to a daughter of the Nasu clan when he was only three years old. He participated in his first combat at [[:en:Battle of Hitotoribashi|Battle of Hitotoribashi]] in 1586, just before he turned 15. [[:en:Battle of Imafuku|Battle of Imafuku]] (1614) }} {{nihongo|'''Satake Yoshinobu'''|佐竹 義宣||August 17, 1570 – March 5, 1633}} was a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' in [[Sengoku period]] and early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] Japan under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and the 19th head of the [[Satake clan]] and 1st ''daimyō'' of [[:en:Kubota Domain|Kubota Domain]] in [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]]. His [[:en:Courtesy title|Courtesy title]] was ''[[:en:Kokushi (officials)|Sakon-no-e-shōshō]]'', later ''[[Daijō-kan|Ukyō-dayū]]'' and ''Jijū'' and his [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Court Rank]] was Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade.{{cite book |last1=Shichinomiya |first1=Keimi |title=常陸・秋田 佐竹一族 |date=2001 |publisher=Shinjinbutsu Oraisha |isbn=440402911X}} ==Biography== Yoshinobu was the eldest son of [[Satake Yoshishige]], and his mother was the daughter of [[:en:Date Harumune|Date Harumune]], which made him the cousin of [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]]. He was born at Ota Castle in [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]]. His early life was that of constant warfare. The Satake clan were fighting the Nasu clan to the north, and as a condition for peace, Yoshinobu was engaged to a daughter of the Nasu clan when he was only three years old. He participated in his first combat at [[:en:Battle of Hitotoribashi|Battle of Hitotoribashi]] in 1586, just before he turned 15. His father officially retired some time in 1589, turning the portion of clan chieftain over to Yoshinobu. During this period, the clan was threatened from the north by [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]] and from the south by [[:en:Hōjō Ujinao|Hōjō Ujinao]]; however, because he quickly pledged allegiance to [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] during the [[Siege of Odawara (1590)]], he was able to retain his landholdings. Under Hideyoshi's banner, he was counted among the six greatest generals of the [[Toyotomi clan]], together with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], [[:en:Maeda Toshiie|Maeda Toshiie]], [[Shimazu Yoshihiro]], [[Mōri Terumoto]], and [[Uesugi Kagekatsu]]. During the siege of Odawara he was also able to extend his control to cover the over the whole of [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]] (with the exception of the territory of the [[Yūki clan]]), by defeating various supporters of the Hōjō, so that by the end of the campaign his ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' was Ranked at over 350,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''. Yoshinobu enjoyed good relations with [[Ishida Mitsunari]], whom he has assisted at the [[Siege of Oshi]] in 1590. He relocated his seat to [[:en:Mito Castle|Mito Castle]], and contributed a large number of troops to Hideyoshi's subjugation of the [[Tohoku region]], and a much smaller number of troops to Hideyoshi's [[:en:Japanese invasions of Korea (1592–1598)|Korean invasion]], although he remained behind at [[Nagoya Castle (Hizen Province)|Nagoya Castle]] and later at [[:en:Fushimi Castle|Fushimi Castle]] with Hideyoshi and did not cross over to the continent. Under Hideyoshi, his ''kokudaka'' gradually was increased to 545,765 ''koku''. At the time of the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] the Satake clan was ordered by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] to participate in the campaign to destroy [[Uesugi Kagekatsu]] in [[:en:Aizu|Aizu]] and to provide a hostage. The Satake clan was divided, with [[Satake Yoshishige]] and many of the samurai strongly supporting Ieyasu and the Eastern Army, whereas Yoshinobu was hesitant, based on his long ties with Ishida Mitsunari and a secret treaty with the [[Uesugi clan]]. Instead of sending his army, he sent a small number of horsemen as reinforcements to [[Tokugawa Hidetada]]'s attack on the [[Sanada clan]], but for the most part remained in Mito attempting to stay neutral in the conflict. With the victory of the pro-Tokugawa forces at the Battle of Sekigahara and the subsequent establishment of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]], the Satake clan was punished by a reduction in their ''kokudaka'' to 180,000 ''koku'', and by being ordered to surrender their ancestral home territory in Hitachi Province for the new [[:en:Kubota Domain|Kubota Domain]], in [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]].Saga, pp. 16–17. The Satake clan also was a direct descendant of the [[Minamoto clan]] and thus had a more legitimate hereditary claim to the title of ''shōgun'' than the [[Tokugawa clan]], which was another reason why Tokugawa Ieyasu viewed the clan with suspicion and as a potential threat. After arriving at their new domain in 1603, the Satake immediately had to face a [[Rokugō rebellion|rebellion]] of local ''[[rōnin]]'' loyal to [[Onodera Yoshimichi]], the former master of the region, which they defeated relatively quickly.{{sfnp|Turnbull|2010|pp=54, 55}} Subsequently, Yoshinobu took steps to gain favor with the new Tokugawa government by actively participating in the [[Siege of Osaka]], fighting Toyotomi commanders such as [[:en:Kimura Shigenari|Kimura Shigenari]] and [[:en:Gotō Matabei|Gotō Matabei]] at the [[:en:Battle of Imafuku|Battle of Imafuku]]. Yoshinobu was married to Shōdō-in, the daughter of Nasu Suketane, to whom he had been engaged as a child. After her death, he remarried to a daughter of Tagaya Shigetsune of [[Shimotsuma Domain]]. He had no children by the first wife and both sons of the second marriage died in infancy. He also had a concubine, the daughter of Ashina Morioki. Yoshinobu adopted two sons as possible heirs. The eldest was the 5th son of Satake Yoshishige, Satake Yoshinao (1612-1656), who at the age of 14, fell asleep during a ''sarugaki'' performance at [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]], and was thus disinherited. he later became a monk. The younger was [[Satake Yoshitaka (1609–1672)|Satake Yoshitaka]], the son of Iwaki Sadataka of [[:en:Kameda Domain|Kameda Domain]], who became his successor. Yoshinobu died at the domain's Edo residence in [[:en:Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda]] in 1633. ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} ==Bibliography== * Saga, Jun'ichi (1987). ''Memories of Silk and Straw: A Self-Portrait of Small-Town Japan''. New York: Kodansha International. *{{cite book|last= Turnbull|first= Stephen|author-link= Stephen Turnbull (historian)|title= Hatamoto. Samurai Horse and Foot Guards 1540–1724|date=2010|publisher= [[Osprey Publishing]]|location= [[Oxford]]|isbn= 978-184603-478-7}} ==Further reading== {{People of the Sengoku period|state=autocollapse}} {{Authority control}}]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Tozama daimyo]] [[Category:1570 births]] [[Category:1633 deaths]] [[Category:Satake clan]] [[Category:Toyotomi retainers]] [[Category:People of Sengoku-period Japan]] [[Category:People of Edo-period Japan]] }} [[file:Satake.jpg|40px]] [[sn:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[en:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Satake Yoshinobu}} [[el:Σατάκε Γιοσινόμπου]] [[es:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[fa:ساتاکه یوشینوبو]] [[fr:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[it:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ja:佐竹義宣 (右京大夫)]] [[ko:사타케 요시노부 (1570년)]] [[pt:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ro:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[ru:Сатакэ Ёсинобу]] [[tr:Satake Yoshinobu]] [[zh:佐竹義宣]]
